title: Girlfriends

jokes.text: Bret's pre-emptive excuse to avoid a coffee shop: 'I don't like croissants'
jokes.setup: Jemaine pressures Bret to come into a café with him. Bret needs a reason to refuse.
jokes.punchline: Bret's stated reason is that he doesn't like croissants — an irrelevant deflection since he's not buying one, and is immediately questioned on it.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dry opener establishing Bret's oblivious social anxiety. The non sequitur logic — 'I don't like croissants' as a reason not to accompany someone buying a croissant — is low-key absurdist and completely character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I don't like croissants.

jokes.text: The looping 'What are you talking about?' exchange where neither character can follow the other's logic
jokes.setup: Jemaine questions Bret about the croissant remark. Both become confused about what the other is even referring to.
jokes.punchline: The dialogue spirals into mutual incomprehension — each asking 'what are you talking about?' — until Bret repeats his original non-point completely unchanged.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully dry Kiwi-deadpan exchange that establishes the show's distinctive register. The circular logic — both men confused about what the other means, resolved by Bret simply repeating himself — is specific to their odd chemistry.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You've gotta come in there with me. I can't go in there by myself and buy a croissant, can I? She'll think I'm weird.

jokes.text: Bret's two-hour pre-negotiation: 'We've been out here talking about it for two hours now.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine tells Bret it'll only take five minutes.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine reveals they've already spent two hours standing outside discussing whether to go in.
jokes.explanation: Extremely well-structured deadpan. The absurdity of two adults spending two hours outside a café debating entry is delivered without irony, which is exactly the show's voice. Economic, character-specific, and a strong laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Stop comparing everything to 'Top Gun.' It's not... this situation's nothing like 'Top Gun.'

jokes.text: The wingman coaching goes immediately wrong: 'Yes, that's true, he would.' / 'He's very popular.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks Bret to be subtle wingman support — just agree with him casually.
jokes.punchline: Bret immediately over-volunteers literal confirmations ('Yes, that's true, he would' / 'He's very popular') that are bizarre and transparent to the café worker.
jokes.explanation: Excellent sitcom construction. The setup asks for subtlety; the punchline delivers maximum unsubtlety. 'He's very popular' as a wingman line to a total stranger is perfectly chosen — both useless and oddly formal. The escalating failure is funny on rewatch because you can see exactly where Bret will go wrong the moment the instructions are given.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We've been out here talking about it for two hours now.

jokes.text: Jemaine spontaneously breaks into French: 'Je voudrais un croissant' — to a bewildered café worker
jokes.setup: The wingman plan has already gone awkwardly. Jemaine is trying to seem impressive.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine inexplicably addresses the American café worker in French, as if this is the obviously correct move.
jokes.explanation: Perfect logic-of-the-scene absurdism. Jemaine's attempt to romanticize a croissant purchase by invoking French culture is both desperate and completely his own. The café worker's single baffled 'What?' is all the commentary needed.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, that's true, he would.

jokes.text: Murray observes the two women chatting: 'They're having a nice conversation.' / 'Do you like having nice conversations?' / 'No. Nope.'
jokes.setup: The guys are watching their prospective dates talking to each other at a party/bar.
jokes.punchline: Murray asks if they enjoy nice conversations. Both flatly say no.
jokes.explanation: A pleasant, light bit of Kiwi deadpan. The comic contract is clear — Murray tries to make something of nothing, gets an honest deflating answer. Functional but not distinguished.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He's very popular.

jokes.text: The girlfriend mix-up: 'I was talking to Lisa... I ended up talking to Felicia, when I should be talking to Lisa.' / 'You ARE talking to Lisa.' / '...Right, well that's obviously what's happened. We should swap.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine confides that a wallet-drop caused a 'disastrous mix-up' where he ended up talking to the wrong woman.
jokes.punchline: Bret reveals Jemaine is already talking to the right woman, Lisa — but Jemaine's response is to immediately absorb this and conclude they should swap anyway.
jokes.explanation: Tight structure and very funny. The 'disastrous mix-up' setup makes Jemaine sound dramatically confused; the reveal that he's already in the right conversation deflates it perfectly. But the real joke is 'Right, well that's obviously what's happened. We should swap' — Jemaine refuses to update his worldview and doubles down on a plan that made no sense even before the correction.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Be more subtle about it.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Mmm, that's true.

jokes.text: 'Oh look, Bret's feeling Felicia's breast.' / 'I'm Felicia. That's Lisa.' / 'Feeling Lisa's breast then, probably.' / 'Yeah.' / 'That must be nice for her.'
jokes.setup: From a distance, Jemaine narrates what he observes Bret doing with one of the women.
jokes.punchline: Felicia corrects the name. Jemaine calmly adjusts his narration without any register shift, then offers 'That must be nice for her' — entirely deadpan commentary on a mildly sexual moment.
jokes.explanation: 'That must be nice for her' is a near-perfect line. The anthropological detachment — observing a friend groping someone like a nature documentarian — is entirely Jemaine's voice. The correction and re-narration ('Feeling Lisa's breast then, probably') adds the extra beat that makes it land harder. Economy and character integration are both excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Je voudrais un croissant.

jokes.text: Bret tries to leave the date early, gets overruled by his own date and then misidentifies her
jokes.setup: Bret announces he's tired and suggests leaving; Jemaine resists, then the woman Bret calls 'Felicia' corrects him — 'That's Lisa, I'm Felicia.'
jokes.punchline: Bret is trying to ditch his own girlfriend on their first date, being overruled by both her and Jemaine, and still gets her name wrong.
jokes.explanation: Serviceable cringe comedy. The name confusion echoes the earlier mix-up joke and reinforces that neither man has the slightest idea what's happening romantically. Doesn't quite land as hard as the cleaner beats.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Non.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Bret calls Felicia 'Lisa' while complimenting her suggestion; she corrects him — 'That's Lisa, I'm Felicia'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has been confusing the girls; now Bret does it too
jokes.punchline: The name confusion is a recurring issue for both men — neither can keep track of which girl is which
jokes.explanation: Extends the running joke about both men being unable to tell the girls apart. Functional rather than exceptional.

dialogue.line: They're having a nice conversation.
Yep.
Do you like having nice conversations?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Nope.

jokes.text: Murray's band manager meeting: 'Bands shouldn't have girlfriends. What about Wham? You never saw Wham with girlfriends. That's how they kept the women wanting them.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine report they have girlfriends at the band meeting. Murray objects on strategic grounds.
jokes.punchline: Murray invokes Wham! as the model of successful girlfriend-avoidance strategy — in order to maintain female fan interest. The logic is simultaneously applied to a band with two members and approximately zero female fans.
jokes.explanation: Murray is the show's richest comedic engine and this is a prime example. The Wham! logic works because Murray applies music industry wisdom to a band that has never had a female fan base to protect. The deadpan delivery of 'That's how they kept the women wanting them' — as insider knowledge — is beautifully misplaced confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There's been a big mix-up, Bret. A disastrous mix-up.

jokes.text: Murray immediately asks about their romantic progress after lecturing them to break up with the girlfriends
jokes.setup: Murray has just firmly advised them to not have girlfriends. 'Okay?' / 'Okay.'
jokes.punchline: Murray immediately pivots: 'So how far did you get with your girlfriend?'
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Murray's authoritative lecture and his instant nosiness is perfectly structured. The one-beat pause between 'Okay?' and the question is the joke. Classic character irony.

dialogue.line: You are talking to Lisa.
Am I?
Yeah.
Right, well that's obviously what's happened. We should swap.

jokes.text: Murray shares his own romantic history unprompted: 'I've told you about how far I've been with Shelly, last Christmas Eve.' / 'Yeah, I don't want to hear that again.'
jokes.setup: Murray deflects the question about himself by immediately offering to share his own sexual history instead.
jokes.punchline: Both guys shut him down — 'I don't want to hear that again' implies he has told this story before at band meetings, and is apparently known for doing so.
jokes.explanation: Good Murray characterization — the lonely manager inserting his own life into band business. 'Last Christmas Eve' as the specific date implies it's his most recent romantic milestone and also the only one. Solid.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, look, Bret's feeling Felicia's breast.

dialogue.speaker: Felicia
dialogue.line: Uh, I'm Felicia. That's...

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes. ...Lisa. Feeling Lisa's breast then, probably.

jokes.text: 'What about you, Jemaine?' / 'Um, I saw Bret feeling that lady's breast.'
jokes.setup: Murray asks Jemaine what he got up to with his girlfriend.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine reports only what he observed happening to Bret — as if his own activity all evening was watching Bret.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback that pays off the earlier Jemaine-as-detached-narrator joke. Also implies Jemaine accomplished nothing of his own on the date. The economy is great — one sentence does all the work.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm actually feeling a little bit tired though. I think maybe we should go.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Murray's 'good news': his car broke down in the ghetto, he met A.J. Jones (brother of Quincy Jones), who offered a high-end stereo for $50
jokes.setup: Murray announces he has good news. The news: he met a music industry contact through his car breaking down.
jokes.punchline: The contact is the BROTHER of Quincy Jones, and the music industry connection is a $50 stolen-goods stereo sale — which Murray presents as a legitimate business deal.
jokes.explanation: Murray's oblivious gullibility is perfectly calibrated here. The escalating degrees of separation — not Quincy Jones, his BROTHER, through a chance meeting under an overpass — and Murray's complete sincerity about its importance is rich character comedy. Also sets up the sawdust payoff at episode end.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You'll lose your female fan base. What about Wham? You never saw Wham with girlfriends.

jokes.text: 'Off the Wall.' / 'Yeah, whoa. I'll say he is. He's crazy. Off the planet. Isn't he going to freeze himself?'
jokes.setup: Bret says Michael Jackson's album 'Off the Wall' — referencing Quincy Jones's production credits.
jokes.punchline: Murray takes 'Off the Wall' as a character assessment of Michael Jackson and riffs on it literally: 'Off the planet. Isn't he going to freeze himself?'
jokes.explanation: Nice bit of wordplay/literalism from Murray. 'Freeze himself' references the cryogenics rumors about MJ with enough obliqueness to feel like genuine Murray rather than a punchline. Pleasant but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've told you about how far I've been with... you know, Shelly, last Christmas Eve.

jokes.text: 'It's girls and spaghetti. We love girls and spaghetti.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine argues Bret should go out tonight because the girls called.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's motivational pitch — 'girls and spaghetti' — is both perfectly reasonable and somehow the most unromantic possible framing for a date.
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable. 'Girls and spaghetti' reduces romance to logistics in a way that's very Flight of the Conchords. The 'We love girls and spaghetti' follow-up treats them as perfectly equivalent pleasures. Short, punchy, and oddly charming.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Um, I saw Bret feeling that lady's breast.

jokes.text: Bret worries about seeming easy: 'I don't want her to think that I'm easy.' / Jemaine: 'You want her to think you're more difficult?'
jokes.setup: Bret inverts conventional gender-role anxiety about sexual availability, applying it to himself.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's response accepts the premise entirely and just asks the logical next question — the humor comes from treating Bret's concern as reasonable while exposing how little sense it makes.
jokes.explanation: Clean and smart. Bret's gender-role reversal is played completely straight; Jemaine's Socratic follow-up ('You want her to think you're more difficult?') is the ideal response. Well-structured with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: My car broke down in the ghetto. That's not the good news,

jokes.text: 'Do you think I dress too provocatively?'
jokes.setup: Bret has been worrying about seeming easy. The gender-role inversion continues.
jokes.punchline: Bret asks the classic question usually attributed to women anxious about sexual objectification, applying it to his own outfit with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: A great one-line character beat. The joke is that Bret is fully inhabiting the gender-reversed anxious-about-modesty role, but delivered as a genuine worry rather than a wink at the audience. The 'No' reply (presumably a visual gag — Bret is wearing something completely unremarkable) makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: He said he could sell me a high-end stereo system for $50.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Bret's 'too provocative' outfit is presumably completely normal; Jemaine says 'you're showing a little too much arm' / 'You look a little cheap'
jokes.setup: Bret asks if he dresses too provocatively. The audience can see him.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine agrees he's showing too much arm — treating an entirely ordinary level of arm-exposure as scandalous — then walks it back with 'You don't look cheap, Bret. You look fine.'
jokes.explanation: Works best as a visual joke — the disparity between Bret's entirely normal appearance and the Victorian modesty debate is funny in context. Jemaine briefly validating the concern ('a little cheap') before correcting himself is a nice beat.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'll say he is. He's crazy. Off the planet. Isn't he going to freeze himself?

jokes.text: 'Wear something a little sluttier. Give her something to look at.' (Jemaine's advice)
jokes.setup: Jemaine is giving Bret advice on how to dress for the date, continuing the gender-role reversal.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine gives advice typically directed at women — 'wear something sluttier, give her something to look at' — completely unselfconsciously, and Bret seems to take it under genuine consideration.
jokes.explanation: Continues the gender-role reversal thread well. Less sharp than the individual beats but serves the running theme.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? It's girls and spaghetti. We love girls and spaghetti.

jokes.text: The bear metaphor: 'You don't want the bear to go completely hungry, you don't want it to attack, so give it a little bait, keep it interested.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine advises Bret on how much 'arm' to show.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine explains the strategy using an elaborate wildlife metaphor — the woman is a bear that must be teased with bait — delivered with complete seriousness.
jokes.explanation: Nicely absurdist. The bear metaphor is both genuinely coherent as advice-logic and completely bizarre as applied to 'how much arm to show.' Jemaine delivering it with total gravity is the key.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I don't want to go too fast, you know. I don't want her to think that I'm easy.

jokes.text: 'The sink works. It just comes out of a different place.'
jokes.setup: Bret tells Murray to fix the sink (again). Murray defends himself.
jokes.punchline: Murray's defense is that the sink technically works — it just dispenses water from an unintended location. This is presented as a reasonable position.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the sink running gag is perfectly crafted. 'It just comes out of a different place' is a sublime defense — it acknowledges the problem entirely while arguing it doesn't constitute a problem. The logic is airtight within Murray's worldview. Highly quotable, brilliantly economical, and a great callback payoff. Justifies the 8.5: this is the kind of line that makes viewers immediately repeat it.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do you think I dress too provocatively?

jokes.text: A.J. Jones quotes the stereo price as $80 ('Good, 80 bucks') after Murray agreed to $50
jokes.setup: Murray agreed to buy the stereo for $50 and is meeting A.J. to close the deal.
jokes.punchline: A.J. calmly announces the price is now $80 with no explanation.
jokes.explanation: Classic scammer-bait-and-switch played with complete straightfacedness. Murray accepts this without protest, which is funnier than the price change itself. Serviceable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I think you're showing a little too much arm.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Am I?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Mmm. Yeah, you look a little cheap.

jokes.text: 'Some shit went down.' / 'Oh really? Okay, sorry to hear about shit going down.'
jokes.setup: Murray explains he doesn't have the money on him.
jokes.punchline: A.J. says 'some shit went down.' Murray responds with formal politeness: 'Sorry to hear about shit going down' — as if this is normal business-meeting language.
jokes.explanation: Murray's formality colliding with street slang is funny here. 'Sorry to hear about shit going down' has the rhythm of professional condolence applied to vague criminal activity. Good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Women don't objectify men in that way, Bret.

jokes.text: 'You need any hot pants? I got a shitload of hot pants.'
jokes.setup: The stereo deal has gone sideways. A.J. pivots to another business proposition.
jokes.punchline: A.J. casually reveals he has a massive surplus of hot pants and is looking to move them, treating this as an entirely natural pivot in a music industry negotiation.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'hot pants' as the overflow inventory is what makes this work — it's not generic contraband, it's a very particular item. Murray's apparent consideration of the offer is the required beat.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: The sink works. It just comes out of a different place.

jokes.text: A.J. hears Murray's demo ('Who likes to rock the party? He likes to rock the party') and immediately says 'I love it'
jokes.setup: Murray nervously plays a demo for A.J., hoping for Quincy Jones's brother's honest opinion.
jokes.punchline: A.J.'s reaction is instant, effusive, and suspicious — 'Can I tell you my honest opinion? I love it' — before pivoting to his own CD-single manufacturing business.
jokes.explanation: The scam is fully readable by the audience but Murray can't see it. The 'honest opinion' setup makes the enthusiasm land as a con. The immediate pivot to a new business scheme is nicely escalating.

dialogue.speaker: A.J.
dialogue.line: You need any hot pants? I got a shitload of hot pants.

jokes.text: 'A little muscley.' (Jemaine's only comment after saying goodbye to Felicia)
jokes.setup: The four of them have said goodbye; Bret and Lisa leave together, Felicia gets a cab. Jemaine watches Felicia go.
jokes.punchline: After a moment, Jemaine's sole observation about this woman is 'A little muscley' — an oddly specific and mildly critical physical assessment delivered flatly into the silence.
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfectly placed. The long beat of watching Felicia walk away, followed only by 'A little muscley', is classic Jemaine Clement comedy — it's critical without being nasty, oddly specific, and tells you everything about his ambivalence. The economy score of 10 is justified: maximum information per syllable.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Who likes to rock the party?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He likes to rock the party.

jokes.text: Bret asks to come up for tea; Lisa says it's a small flat; Bret asks Jemaine 'How long do you wait before letting a girl come upstairs?' / Jemaine: 'Three years.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks to come up for tea. Lisa deflects. Bret turns to Jemaine for advice on the right waiting period.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine answers with total certainty: 'Three years.'
jokes.explanation: The question is already funny — Bret asking how long you wait before letting A GIRL come into YOUR place, when he's the one trying to get into HER flat. But 'Three years' is the perfect answer: an absurdly specific and long time delivered with the confidence of hard-won wisdom. Also foreshadows Bret's defensiveness about intimacy.

dialogue.speaker: Felicia
dialogue.line: A little muscley.

jokes.text: 'What... what do you think I am, dangerous?' (Bret, after Lisa reacts to the three-year rule with 'Oh, you're serious.')
jokes.setup: Lisa is surprised he's serious about the three-year rule. Bret has a moment of insecurity.
jokes.punchline: Bret asks if Lisa thinks he's dangerous — as if that's the only possible reason she'd be cautious about him.
jokes.explanation: Nice self-flattering misread — Bret interprets caution as recognition of his dangerousness rather than as normal hesitancy. Good Bret-specific logic.

dialogue.line: Bret, how long do you wait until you let a girl come upstairs?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Three years.

jokes.text: Lisa's cab excuse: 'I can't get a cab to Brooklyn this time of night.' / Bret: 'Uh, I could give you directions to the F train.' / Lisa: 'That's a really dangerous stop. I've heard there's maniacs down there.' / Bret: 'Maniacs?'
jokes.setup: Lisa wants to stay over. She uses the late-cab excuse. Bret helpfully offers subway directions.
jokes.punchline: Lisa immediately escalates to 'maniacs at the F train stop' — and Bret is visibly concerned by this new information, which makes him reconsider.
jokes.explanation: Good cringe escalation — Lisa is nakedly manufacturing reasons to stay; Bret is nakedly trying to give her an out. The 'maniacs' detail is the right level of absurd. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh well, I mean I'm... I'm not gonna be able to get a cab back to Brooklyn this time of night, so...

jokes.text: The sleeping arrangements negotiation: 'I'll sleep on the couch and you can sleep on my bed.' / 'I'll be in your bed.' / 'Okay.'
jokes.setup: Lisa has maneuvered her way into staying. The arrangement is discussed.
jokes.punchline: Lisa summarizes the agreed outcome — 'I'll be in your bed' — with a tone of casual satisfaction, while Bret apparently sleeps on his own couch in his own flat.
jokes.explanation: The scene-end beat of 'I'll be in your bed. Okay.' is the right capper. Lisa's dominance of the negotiation is well-played. Not exceptional but serves the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: that's a really dangerous stop. I mean, I... I've heard there's maniacs down there.

jokes.text: The negotiation over couch vs. bed: 'I'll be in your bed. / Okay. / Okay. / Good night. / Good night.' — followed by Bret promptly appearing in her bedroom.
jokes.setup: Lisa offers Bret the couch; he volunteers to sleep on it while she sleeps in her own bed
jokes.punchline: The arrangement is a verbal pretzel that ends with Bret somehow in her bed, and he announces this as a settled fact: 'I'll be in your bed.'
jokes.explanation: Bret's logic somehow produces 'I'll be in your bed' from a conversation where he offered to sleep on the couch. The deadpan 'Okay... okay. Good night. Good night.' acceptance is funny in its normalcy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: A Kiss is Not a Contract

jokes.text: Lisa sneaks up to Bret at night: 'Could I just sleep next to you? I promise I won't do anything.' / 'No.' / 'Come on, just give me a little sugar.' / 'Definitely no sugar.'
jokes.setup: Bret is asleep on the floor/couch. Lisa comes to him with the classic sleepover negotiation.
jokes.punchline: The gender reversal is now complete — Lisa is doing the 'just sleep next to you, I promise' negotiation that is conventionally male. Bret's 'Definitely no sugar' is his attempt at a firm boundary.
jokes.explanation: The gender-reversal is consistent and well-played. 'Definitely no sugar' is a good line — it tries to be firm while using the same diminutive language Lisa is using. The setup for the song that follows.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: # Doesn't mean it's all about poontang #

jokes.text: THE 'A KISS IS NOT A CONTRACT' SONG — full musical number
jokes.setup: Lisa invokes 'the unspoken rule' — that kissing all evening means Bret owes her sex. Bret breaks into song to explain his position.
jokes.punchline: The song is a formally beautiful, heartfelt ballad arguing that consent doesn't compound. The comedy comes from (1) the earnestness of the musical style vs. the subject matter; (2) specific lyrical choices ('tonsil hockey,' 'the goal that's in my jockey,' 'pants paradise'); (3) 'Just because I'm in a two-man novelty band / Doesn't mean it's all about poontang'; (4) 'They call it a fly because it takes you up to heaven'; (5) the entire episode's gender reversal made explicit in song form.
jokes.explanation: The episode's centerpiece and one of the series' great songs. The joke operates on every level simultaneously: (1) Consent-awareness content delivered as a lush romantic ballad — the tonal dissonance is perfect; (2) Lyrics are both genuinely clever and absurd: 'just because we've been playing tonsil hockey / doesn't mean you get to score the goal that's in my jockey' is intricate and funny; (3) 'Just because I'm in a two-man novelty band / Doesn't mean it's all about poontang' — the self-aware reference to their actual band status is brilliant meta-comedy; (4) 'They call it a fly because it takes you up to heaven' is the best single line in the episode — an absurd folk-etymology pun delivered with acoustic sincerity; (5) The song earns its emotional beats while being ridiculous throughout. This is what the show does better than anyone: write genuinely good songs that are also genuinely funny. Scores 9.5 on impact because it's the kind of song that makes audiences immediately look up the lyrics.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: They call it a fly because it takes you up to heaven

jokes.text: 'Just because I'm in a two-man novelty band / Doesn't mean it's all about poontang'
jokes.setup: Mid-song, Bret catalogues reasons why kissing doesn't imply obligation.
jokes.punchline: The song unexpectedly name-drops the characters' own real-world status ('two-man novelty band') as a premise — acknowledging that being in the band might create certain expectations, and firmly rejecting them.
jokes.explanation: This lyric deserves its own entry because it's doing multiple things at once: it's self-aware ('novelty band'), it's absurd (the implication that being in a novelty band would create sexual expectations), and 'poontang' is an inspired word choice — funny in itself, and the contrast between the folk-ballad register and the word is perfect. One of the episode's most quotable moments.

dialogue.line: They call it a fly because it takes you up to heaven

jokes.text: 'They call it a fly because it takes you up to heaven'
jokes.setup: Bret is explaining, with mounting rhetorical effort, why a kiss creates no obligation.
jokes.punchline: He produces a folk etymology for the word 'fly' (as in trouser fly) — that it's called a 'fly' because it 'takes you up to heaven' — delivered with the confidence of genuine insight.
jokes.explanation: The single best line in the episode. A completely invented etymological claim — obviously false, entirely plausible as Bret's logic — delivered as sincere folk wisdom in a musical context. The wordplay is elegant: 'fly' as insect (flies), 'fly' as trouser opening, 'fly' as airborne (to heaven). It's a triple pun that doesn't feel labored because it arrives in song. The 'Oh whoa' that follows suggests even Bret is impressed by himself. This line alone justifies the episode. 9.3 craft, 9.5 impact.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, it turns out she's very aggressive.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Did she hit you?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, she wanted to go all the way.

jokes.text: 'I'm only one man' / 'We're only two men, ladies'
jokes.setup: The song has been Bret's solo argument; it ends with a group appeal
jokes.punchline: The correction from 'I'm only one man' to 'we're only two men' — seemingly from both of them — implies Jemaine has joined in despite not being in this scene, and directly addresses 'ladies' as if to an audience
jokes.explanation: The self-correction from singular to plural is funny in itself, and the direct address to 'ladies' breaks the scene's fiction. The band's identity as a duo being invoked even in a personal romantic song is good meta-comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I thought you already did go all the way.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, now she wants to have sex.

jokes.text: 'Well, it turns out she's very aggressive.' / 'Did she hit you?' / 'No, she wanted to go all the way.' / 'Mmm.' / 'I thought you already did go all the way.' / 'No, now she wants to have sex.'
jokes.setup: Bret reports that something disturbing happened — Lisa was 'very aggressive.'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's assumption is physical violence; Bret clarifies it's sexual interest. Then: Jemaine assumed Bret had already had sex; Bret clarifies they went 'all the way' to some pre-sex threshold he considers the full distance.
jokes.explanation: A double misdirection that works cleanly. First: 'aggressive' → Jemaine expects violence → it's sexual interest. Second: 'all the way' → Jemaine assumes sex → Bret's 'all the way' stops short of sex by his own definition. Two good subversions in quick succession, with Jemaine's flat 'Mmm' between them being the straight-man gold standard.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She jumped on top of me and then touched me on the penis.

jokes.text: 'What gave you that impression?' / 'She jumped on top of me and then touched me on the penis.' / 'Oh I see. You're lying.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine questions how Bret could have gotten the impression Lisa wanted sex.
jokes.punchline: Bret gives an extremely specific and unambiguous answer. Jemaine's response — 'Oh I see. You're lying' — implies he simply doesn't believe this happened, rather than accepting it.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's 'Oh I see. You're lying.' is a great deadpan response. After a completely unambiguous explanation of sexual intent, Jemaine's instinct is disbelief rather than acceptance — which reveals his low opinion of Bret's romantic prospects without stating it. The flatness of delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's like that scene from 'Top Gun.' You know your favorite scene in 'Top Gun'?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I don't know that movie.

jokes.text: 'It's like that scene from Top Gun. You know your favorite scene in Top Gun?' / 'I don't know that movie.' / 'Yeah, it's your favorite scene.'
jokes.setup: Bret makes a Top Gun reference to describe the sexual encounter.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine denies knowing the movie. Bret insists it's his favorite scene.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the episode's opening beat. The running gag of Top Gun references now has an ironic twist — Bret is making the reference this time, and Jemaine denies knowing the film he previously criticized Bret for referencing too much.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: She gave me this free day-old croissant.

jokes.text: 'Well, I just said I'm not ready to have sex.' / 'And that worked?' / 'No, that didn't work at all, 'cause then she wanted to have sex.' / 'Oh. It worked then, good.'
jokes.setup: Bret explains he told Lisa he wasn't ready. Jemaine asks if it worked.
jokes.punchline: Bret confirms it didn't work — and then Jemaine hears 'she still wanted sex' as a success outcome, congratulating him.
jokes.explanation: Sharp structure. 'No, that didn't work at all, 'cause then she wanted to have sex' / 'Oh. It worked then, good.' — Jemaine's definition of 'working' is simply 'she still wanted sex' which is true. The reframe is quick, clean, and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Hot bod, gross face. I get it. Just hit that shit from behind.

jokes.text: 'I think Felicia's pretty keen too. She gave me this free day-old croissant.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine reflects on Felicia's interest in him.
jokes.punchline: The evidence Jemaine cites for Felicia being 'pretty keen' is a free day-old croissant — the lowest-value possible gift, and also a callback to the episode's opening.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that rewards attentive viewers. The croissant from the opening becomes a symbol of romantic interest — but it's day-old and free, which is both pathetically low-key and somehow charming. Character-specific because only Jemaine would cite this as significant evidence.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: How do you think I eat so well every lunchtime?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Hey, Mrs. Chang.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Thanks for the egg rolls, huh.

jokes.text: The 'Conchord CDs' vendor being turned down: 'Out of my area.'
jokes.setup: Someone is selling the band's CDs on the street. A passerby is offered one.
jokes.punchline: 'Out of my area' — the CD being hawked on the street is declined as if it were a door-to-door service pitch.
jokes.explanation: A brief visual/street gag. 'Out of my area' as a rejection of a CD is mildly funny — treating unsolicited music like an unwanted utility service. Light but functional.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Don't you know that if a girl gets aroused and doesn't climax, that she can really damage herself? Bret, I might not be able to have a baby.

jokes.text: 'Hey, Dave, can I ask you a question about women's sexuality?' / 'Sure, you came to the right place for that.'
jokes.setup: Bret seeks advice from Dave the record store clerk.
jokes.punchline: Dave immediately declares himself the right person to consult on women's sexuality — with total confidence, though nothing we've seen about Dave suggests this is true.
jokes.explanation: Sets up Dave as a confident-but-dubious advisor. The confidence is the joke — 'you came to the right place' from a record store employee. Functional character intro for the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Because tomorrow, Bret, I ship out to Iraq.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Iraq?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm in Delta Force, Bret.

jokes.text: 'Hot bod, gross face. I get it. Just hit that shit from behind.'
jokes.setup: Bret describes the girl as someone he's not sure about physically ('she's pretty'). Dave misreads and offers a solution.
jokes.punchline: Dave's advice is crudely practical and entirely misses the point of what Bret is actually dealing with.
jokes.explanation: Dave's advice is intentionally bad, which is the joke, but the line itself isn't particularly inventive. Serves character (Dave as hilariously useless advisor) more than it functions as a standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: Felicia
dialogue.line: I only went out with you in the first place because Lisa kind of like insisted that I be her wingman.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're her wingman?

jokes.text: Bret's romantic ideal: 'It would be flowers, violins, I'd be in a nice suit. Everything would just be perfect.'
jokes.setup: Bret explains why he isn't ready — he has a specific vision for how his first time should be.
jokes.punchline: The vision is extremely traditional and romantic (flowers, violins, a nice suit) — which is both endearing and funny given the situation he's currently in (an aggressive woman chasing him around his own flat).
jokes.explanation: Good character work for Bret — his genuine romantic idealism is sweet and funny simultaneously. 'I'd be in a nice suit' as a specific fantasy detail is the best beat here.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It was over in five minutes. I mean, she fell asleep.

jokes.text: Dave's sausage philosophy: 'In America, if you want something, you've got to be prepared to put out for it. How do you think I eat so well every lunchtime?'
jokes.setup: Dave gives life advice about sexual economics in America.
jokes.punchline: Dave reveals his personal application of this philosophy: he exchanges sexual favors for lunch from Mrs. Chang. The implication is left just ambiguous enough.
jokes.explanation: A well-executed dark comedy beat. Dave's casual revelation that he 'puts out' for egg rolls from Mrs. Chang recontextualizes his entire worldview as grounded in deeply pragmatic personal experience. The 'Hey, Mrs. Chang, thanks for the egg rolls' capper is perfect — mundane and damning simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, Iraq.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Iraq?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes, she's in Delta Force. She's been deployed to Fallujah. But she works in the croissant shop.

jokes.text: How do you think I eat so well every lunchtime? / Hey, Mrs. Chang. Thanks for the egg rolls, huh.
jokes.setup: Dave claims that in America you have to 'put out' for what you want
jokes.punchline: His example of this principle is that he exchanges sexual favors with Mrs. Chang for egg rolls
jokes.explanation: The visual of Mrs. Chang materializing immediately after Dave's speech — implying she's in earshot or proximity — is well-timed. The idea that Dave's sexual philosophy produces egg rolls as its practical output is both absurd and consistent with his character. The callback structure (claim → immediate visual proof) is tight.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She's a pastry chef and a sniper.

jokes.text: Jemaine's silent reaction after Dave's Mrs. Chang reveal
jokes.setup: Dave explains he eats well by 'putting out' and then waves to Mrs. Chang.
jokes.punchline: The transcript shows a pause — Jemaine (or Bret) simply absorbs this information with a beat of silence, then presumably nothing is said. The reaction IS the joke.
jokes.explanation: The 'What?' marked at 18:09 suggests a beat of stunned silence/incomprehension following Dave's Mrs. Chang admission. The show uses this device well — the reaction beat rewards attentive viewers and is best appreciated on rewatch.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: She keeps pressuring me into sex. 'Give me some more sex.' Oh, give me a rest... for five minutes.

jokes.text: Lisa invents a fake medical condition: 'If a girl gets aroused and doesn't climax, she can really damage herself.' / 'I didn't know that.' / 'It's kinda common knowledge.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks Lisa if she cares about him. Lisa pivots to a medical argument for why he must have sex with her.
jokes.punchline: Lisa invents a false medical claim (arousal-without-climax causes damage), Bret is immediately convinced, and Lisa doubles down by calling it 'kinda common knowledge' — implying Bret's ignorance is the real problem.
jokes.explanation: A classic manipulative-partner move applied in reverse gender direction. The fake medical claim is recognizable as a variation on a known piece of manipulation, which gives it a second layer. 'It's kinda common knowledge' is the perfect gaslighting capper — shaming Bret for his ignorance of an invented fact.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Cheer up, Bret. Mmm, it's the talking stove. Things'll work out.

jokes.text: 'Tomorrow, Bret, I ship out to Iraq.' / 'Iraq?' / 'Yeah, I'm in Delta Force, Bret.' / 'You're in Delta Force?' / 'I gotta take one for my country, so the least you could do is take one from you.'
jokes.setup: Lisa has already used fake medicine to pressure Bret. She now escalates to her next argument.
jokes.punchline: Lisa reveals she's shipping out to Iraq with Delta Force tomorrow — a completely implausible claim about a pastry chef — and uses it as a guilt-based sexual leverage argument: 'take one for my country / take one from you.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best escalation jokes. Lisa's manipulation has now reached fully insane heights — she's a croissant shop employee who is also Delta Force, shipping out tomorrow, and invoking patriotic duty as sexual leverage. The phrase 'take one for my country / take one from you' is perfectly constructed — a pun built into a guilt trip. The absurdism is earned because it's the logical escalation of everything Lisa has done. Scores 8.6 craft: the structure (fake medicine → Iraq/Delta Force → patriotic pun) is excellent; the wordplay in the final line is genuinely clever.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: How many have you sold?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: One.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: But I would have liked to have gotten a few more out than that.

jokes.text: Jemaine preemptively tells Felicia he doesn't want to have sex with her; she says she doesn't want to either; Jemaine's reaction: 'Oh. Oh... ooh ooh!'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has prepared a speech about not wanting sex. He delivers it. Felicia immediately agrees she doesn't want to either.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's confident declaration gets the response he asked for, which immediately produces wounded vanity — the 'Oh... ooh ooh!' suggests he expected more resistance.
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in character comedy. Jemaine has prepared the chaste, principled speech; the joke is that getting exactly what he asked for is immediately unsatisfying. The 'Oh. Oh... ooh ooh!' sells it — this is a sound effect of wounded pride, not words. Scores 8.5 because the joke exists entirely in the gap between what Jemaine said he wanted and how he responds to getting it. Highly Jemaine-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: This one's got sawdust in it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Did you buy some sawdust as well?

jokes.text: 'Is it because I'm too dangerous? 'Cause you think I'm just gonna ride out of this town the same way I rode in, and when I go, I'll take your heart with me?'
jokes.setup: Felicia says there are slight concerns about getting involved with Jemaine.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine pitches a romantic-outlaw self-image — the drifter who steals hearts — completely at odds with who he actually is.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's brief self-romanticization as a dangerous drifter is wonderfully character-specific — he's a broke New Zealander who lives above a record store in New York, and his attempt to cast himself as a wandering heartbreaker is both funny and weirdly touching. 'The same way I rode in' is a great phrase for someone who presumably arrived on a budget flight. Scores high on character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Did you want to buy sawdust, though?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No! I bought the CDs, didn't I?

jokes.text: The circular negotiation of mutual non-consent: 'Don't want to.' 'Don't want to.' 'Do not want to.' 'Me too, don't want to.' 'Don't want to' and 'don't want to.' 'Okay, let's not then.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine and Felicia are reaching the conclusion of their conversation about not having sex
jokes.punchline: The conversation becomes a formal negotiation in which both parties repeatedly confirm their position ('don't want to' x5) before reaching the conclusion to not have sex
jokes.explanation: The formalization of mutual disinterest into a negotiation is absurdly funny. Both characters treat this as a complex agreement requiring multiple confirmations. The joke runs slightly long but the final 'okay, let's not then' as a binding resolution is the right button.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, that was just physical. Just physical. It was just... I mean, we were just... we were just using each other, weren't we?

jokes.text: Visible struggle/wrestling as Lisa apparently forces Bret to have sex — inferred from 'Okay, move. / You can move a little more. / Come on.'
jokes.setup: After all of Bret's resistance, Lisa appears to be physically maneuvering him.
jokes.punchline: The lines suggest physical comedy — Bret is being positioned/moved — which is both farcical and a continuation of the gender reversal. 'Come on' from Lisa implies his lack of cooperation.
jokes.explanation: An implied physical comedy moment that works better visually than textually. The short transcript lines suggest a physical gag playing out, and in context it lands as the absurd endpoint of the episode's gender-reversal conceit.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're attracted to bastards.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bastard girls.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's true.

jokes.text: 'Um, Lisa? Could I go home now?'
jokes.setup: Bret is in Lisa's flat after having apparently had sex. He's asking to leave.
jokes.punchline: Bret asks, politely and somewhat plaintively, to go home — as if he needs permission to leave. The wording ('could I') treats the situation as if he's been detained.
jokes.explanation: Possibly the funniest single line in the episode. 'Could I go home now?' after being overwhelmed by an aggressive partner is a perfect gender-reversal payoff — it's the line usually asked by a woman trying to escape a man who won't let her leave. The politeness, the 'could I', the 'now' (implying he's been trying to go for a while) all make it land perfectly. Justifies 8.3 craft: economical, character-specific, perfectly timed, and the purest expression of the episode's central joke.

dialogue.line: Apparently, they're rewritable. So he just deletes the music files and then sells them as blank CDs. Apparently, it's worth more that way so...

jokes.text: Jemaine is in the shower when Bret enters crying; Jemaine: 'Could you please get out of here? I'm having a shower.' / 'Okay. I was in here first. You come in here with your clothes on, crying.'
jokes.setup: Bret has been traumatized by the Lisa encounter; he retreats to the bathroom.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine is already in the shower — and his complaint is not that Bret is upset, but that Bret came into the bathroom 'with your clothes on, crying' — the specific detail of being clothed being noted as the issue.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's specificity about 'with your clothes on' is the right comic detail — it implies Jemaine would be less bothered if Bret entered the shower properly undressed. Character-specific absurdism delivered as a genuine complaint.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I was thinking she was too good for you.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She was actually too good for me.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, a bit too pretty for you.

jokes.text: 'It was over in five minutes. I mean, she fell asleep.'
jokes.setup: Bret reports on what happened with Lisa.
jokes.punchline: Bret's summary of the sexual encounter is 'over in five minutes' — the classic male-inadequacy joke — but then subverted: it was over because SHE fell asleep, not for the usual reason.
jokes.explanation: A nice misdirection. 'Over in five minutes' triggers one expectation (Bret came too quickly), which is then subverted — she fell asleep, implying she lost interest mid-act rather than Bret underperforming in the usual way. The 'I mean' sets up the correction perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm Bret.

jokes.text: 'Are you and Lisa gonna get married now?' / 'I wish, but I don't know. She's got to go to war.' / 'What?' / 'Yeah, Iraq.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks if the sex led anywhere romantically.
jokes.punchline: Bret responds with the Iraq plot, which Jemaine is apparently hearing for the first time and finds as impossible to believe as the audience does.
jokes.explanation: Good callback that re-grounds the absurdist Iraq plot in a new context. Jemaine's 'What?' is the correct audience surrogate response. Maintains the joke without straining it.

jokes.text: 'But she works in the croissant shop.' / 'Yeah, well, she's got two jobs. She's a pastry chef and a sniper.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine points out the obvious problem with Lisa being Delta Force.
jokes.punchline: Bret reconciles the contradiction with complete logic: she has two jobs — pastry chef AND sniper. Both delivered with a straight face.
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. 'She's a pastry chef and a sniper' resolves the absurdist plot by accepting both realities simultaneously, treating the professional combination as unremarkable. The logic is impeccable within the episode's universe and the juxtaposition ('pastry chef / sniper') is genuinely inspired. Justifies 8.9: the line works on first viewing as a laugh and on rewatch as an appreciation of how perfect the word choice is. Both jobs are one syllable too long or too short to be anything but funny.

jokes.text: 'Well, if it makes you feel better, me and Felicia are probably gonna have to break up too. She keeps pressuring me into sex. Give me some more sex. Oh, give me a rest...'
jokes.setup: Bret is moping about Lisa leaving. Jemaine tries to comfort him by sharing his own situation.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine complains that Felicia 'keeps pressuring him into sex' — this directly contradicts the scene we just watched where Felicia made clear she didn't want to and Jemaine was the one disappointed. His revision of the story is a classic defensive male narrative.
jokes.explanation: A great callback reversal. We watched Felicia say 'yuck, no way' and Jemaine be wounded. Now he retrospectively casts himself as the besieged party resisting her advances. The gap between what we saw and what Jemaine is now claiming is the joke — and the specificity of 'Give me some more sex. Oh, give me a rest...' (performing Felicia's alleged complaints) is perfect. High character integration.

jokes.text: Well, if it makes you feel better, me and Felicia are probably gonna have to break up too. She keeps pressuring me into sex. 'Give me some more sex.' Oh, give me a rest...
jokes.setup: Bret laments his impending separation from Lisa; Jemaine offers solidarity
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's complaint is the inverse of Bret's — he's being pressured INTO sex, which is also a problem. 'Give me a rest for five minutes.'
jokes.explanation: The 'give me a rest for five minutes' complaint about too much sex, from a man who has been attempting to meet women all episode, is a good character irony joke. Jemaine imitating Felicia's nagging in a generic American voice is a small extra comedic layer. The five-minute callback to 'It was over in five minutes' (joke 59) may be unintentional but lands as a coincidental bonus.

jokes.text: The talking stove: 'Cheer up, Bret. / Mmm, it's the talking stove. / Things'll work out. / Yeah? / You love the talking stove. / Oh, let's make a cake.'
jokes.setup: Bret is moping. A stove tells him to cheer up.
jokes.punchline: A kitchen appliance is providing emotional support, and rather than being baffled, Bret responds with gentle familiarity — 'it's the talking stove' — as if this is a known comfort. The resolution is 'let's make a cake.'
jokes.explanation: A perfect piece of FOTC absurdism — the talking stove appears without explanation and is treated as an established fixture of emotional life. 'You love the talking stove' implies a backstory of previous interactions. 'Oh, let's make a cake' as a solution to romantic heartbreak is wonderfully non sequitur and oddly comforting. Scores 8.0 for pure absurdist confidence.

jokes.text: Can I have one? / You can have a whole box. There you go. / Really? / Yes, take it home. I can't store them here.
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to move CDs
jokes.punchline: Murray eagerly offers whole boxes of unsold CDs as gifts, his only goal now being to not have to store them
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Murray's desperation to offload boxes of his band's unsold CDs onto band members is consistent and mildly funny.

jokes.text: The boxes of CDs turn out to be sawdust
jokes.setup: Murray has bought 2,500+ CDs from A.J. Jones (brother of Quincy) in a deal conducted under an overpass.
jokes.punchline: Most or all of the boxes contain sawdust. Murray has been completely conned.
jokes.explanation: The episode-long A.J. Jones con plot pays off. The sawdust reveal is well-constructed — Murray only checked one box before distributing them to everyone. The 'It's a lot lighter. Did you pick them up?' exchange is particularly good, as Murray defends not noticing an obvious fraud indicator. A satisfying payoff for patient setup.

jokes.text: 'What, you had sex with him?' (Bret, when Murray describes doing business 'underneath an overpass')
jokes.setup: Murray says 'I did the business underneath an overpass and he was dodgy from the beginning.'
jokes.punchline: Bret hears 'did the business' as a sexual euphemism and asks if Murray had sex with A.J. Jones.
jokes.explanation: A nice double-meaning moment. The episode has been about sex throughout, so Bret's mind going there is in-character. Murray's indignant clarification ('No, he sold me the sawdust boxes!') makes it land.

jokes.text: 'Did you want to buy sawdust, though?'
jokes.setup: Murray is furious about being sold sawdust. Bret is processing the situation.
jokes.punchline: Bret asks if Murray wanted sawdust — as if the issue might simply be a miscommunication about the desired product.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan. 'Did you want to buy sawdust, though?' takes the victim's righteous indignation and casually introduces doubt — maybe Murray did want sawdust and forgot? The question is logically absurd but grammatically innocent, which is perfect Bret. Highly quotable, excellent economy.

jokes.text: 'I should find Quincy Jones and let him know what his brother's up to. He needs to know this.' / 'Oh, I haven't got his number.' / 'You could email him.' / 'I'd love to email him.'
jokes.setup: Murray, stewing over the con, decides to escalate to Quincy Jones himself.
jokes.punchline: The plan to contact Quincy Jones immediately collapses ('haven't got his number') and is salvaged by email — and Murray's 'I'd love to email him' is delivered with genuine enthusiasm, as if emailing Quincy Jones about his con-artist brother were a completely achievable social call.
jokes.explanation: Murray's fantasy of a corrective email to Quincy Jones is pure character comedy. 'I'd love to email him' has the tone of someone planning a reasonable next step in a legitimate business dispute. The logic is impeccable within Murray's worldview.

jokes.text: Lisa bumped into on the street — turns out she didn't ship out after all; 'Oh no. I'm shipping out tomorrow.' (again)
jokes.setup: Bret mopes about Lisa being in Iraq. They run into her.
jokes.punchline: She didn't ship out. She then immediately announces she IS shipping out — tomorrow.
jokes.explanation: The Iraq gag has another beat. The fact that she didn't ship out as stated but still insists she will ship out 'tomorrow' keeps the joke alive without adding much new. Functional callback.

jokes.text: 'But you said you love me.' / 'Oh. You had sex on me.' / 'No, oh yeah, that was just physical. We were just using each other, weren't we?' / 'Just using each other.' / 'Oh, we were just using each other.' / 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Bret expected emotional commitment from their night together; Lisa is now framing it as mutually casual.
jokes.punchline: Bret, clearly hurt, is walked through accepting the 'just using each other' framing, each 'yeah' a small defeat. 'You had sex on me' is Bret's specific complaint about what happened.
jokes.explanation: 'You had sex on me' is a wonderful turn of phrase — both grammatically odd and emotionally expressive. The scene's rhythm of Bret slowly adopting Lisa's framing ('Just using each other' / 'Oh, we were just using each other') is good cringe comedy and good character work.

jokes.text: Jemaine mocks Bret for having said 'I love you': 'I know! You see him? I love you.' (mocking voice)
jokes.setup: Lisa has just brushed off Bret. Jemaine has apparently witnessed or been told about Bret declaring love.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine immediately mocks his best friend to his face with an exaggerated impersonation.
jokes.explanation: Functional beat, mostly character texture. Jemaine's lack of sympathy is funny but not especially clever.

jokes.text: 'Your real problem, Bret, is the kind of girls you go for. You're attracted to bastards. Bastard girls.' / 'It's true.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine offers post-breakup analysis of Bret's romantic patterns.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine diagnoses Bret as attracted to 'bastards' — specifically 'bastard girls' — and Bret agrees.
jokes.explanation: 'Bastard girls' is the right phrase — applying a traditionally male insult to women without irony. Bret's immediate agreement ('It's true') is the correct response. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Murray arrives, assumes they don't have girlfriends: 'I knew you didn't have girlfriends.'
jokes.setup: The girls are gone. Murray arrives and sees them without the women.
jokes.punchline: Murray's immediate conclusion — 'I knew you didn't have girlfriends' — reveals he never believed them, while ignoring that Bret clearly did have a girlfriend and just got dumped.
jokes.explanation: Good Murray beat — his worldview is confirmed in his mind regardless of the actual evidence. The correction ('Yes we... well, Bret did. Yeah, sort of, she didn't really...') from the guys is nicely fumbled.

jokes.text: Murray sold all the CDs to Dave, who uses them as blank rewritable media: 'Apparently they're rewritable. So he just deletes the music files and then sells them as blank CDs. Apparently it's worth more that way.'
jokes.setup: Murray announces he sold ALL the CDs — finally! — to Dave.
jokes.punchline: Dave bought them not for the music but to erase it and resell them as blanks. The music is literally worth less than nothing — it reduces the value of the medium.
jokes.explanation: A devastating and perfectly constructed ending to the CD subplot. The joke has multiple layers: (1) the music is worth negative value; (2) Murray presents this as a reasonable outcome ('They're still getting out there'); (3) the guys' resigned acceptance ('As blanks.' / 'Yeah.' / 'It's good.') is the ideal straight-man response. Murray's 'they're still getting out there' is a monument of managerial delusion. Scores 8.5 craft: elegant structure, deeply character-specific, darkly funny about artistic failure.

jokes.text: 'It's not ideal.' / 'It's not ideal, but yeah, they're still getting out there, aren't they?' / 'Yeah, well, they're blank though.' / 'Yeah, but they'll be out there, and... you know.' / 'As blanks.' / 'Yeah.' / 'It's good.' / 'It's good, I know.'
jokes.setup: Bret points out the obvious problem with Dave's solution.
jokes.punchline: The three of them slowly, mutually agree that selling their music as blank CDs is 'good' — through a series of flat, resigned affirmations that clearly mean the opposite.
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of this exchange is beautifully deadpan — each iteration of 'it's good / yeah / as blanks / yeah / it's good, I know' is a small additional deflation. The comedy is in the communal acceptance of failure delivered without affect. A good example of how FOTC uses minimalism.

jokes.text: 'Just gotta find someone that'll buy this sawdust now, and I'll be complete.'
jokes.setup: Murray wraps up, still holding boxes of sawdust he was conned into buying.
jokes.punchline: Murray's goal for completion is now selling the sawdust — he's moved from music management to sawdust brokerage, and finding a buyer for it will make him whole.
jokes.explanation: 'And I'll be complete' is the perfect ending note for Murray — his sense of professional fulfillment is now entirely detached from music and attached to commodity lumber byproduct. The line has exactly the right formal register: slightly elevated language ('I'll be complete') for a deeply unambitious goal. Good final Murray beat.

jokes.text: 'That girl wasn't right for you.' / 'Yeah, I guess she wasn't really good enough for me.' / 'I was thinking she was too good for you.' / 'She was actually too good for me.' / 'Yes, a bit too pretty for you.' / 'Hmm.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine tries to cheer Bret up about the Lisa breakup.
jokes.punchline: Bret misreads Jemaine's comfort as agreement that Lisa was beneath him. Jemaine corrects: Lisa was TOO GOOD for Bret. Bret accepts this, and Jemaine specifies 'a bit too pretty for you.'
jokes.explanation: A great dialogue beat. Bret's ego-protective misread ('not good enough for me') gets flatly corrected, then helpfully elaborated ('a bit too pretty for you'). The escalation from 'too good for you' to the specific 'too pretty' is nicely brutal. Jemaine's friendship expressed as honest deflation.

jokes.text: A woman approaches Bret: 'Hi. Are you Bret? I'm a friend of Lisa's. I heard you like to have a good time.' / Bret: 'I'm Bret.'
jokes.setup: Bret has just been told he's not good-looking enough for Lisa. He's moping.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's friend approaches immediately with 'I heard you like to have a good time' — Lisa has apparently recommended Bret as a 'good time.' Bret's response ('I'm Bret') is exactly the same identity-confirmation he gave at the start of the episode, now carrying the whole arc.
jokes.explanation: An excellent episode-closing button. The joke works on multiple levels: (1) Lisa has apparently told her friends that Bret is worth pursuing — having been his aggressor, she's now his pimp; (2) 'I heard you like to have a good time' is a knowing innuendo that the whole episode has been building toward; (3) 'I'm Bret' as the final line mirrors his introductory identity confirmation, but now loaded with completely different meaning — he's no longer shy Bret buying a croissant but Bret with a reputation. Structurally satisfying and funny.

tmdb_overview: Bret and Jemaine pick up two women at a croissant shop - but end up with very different dating experiences. Meanwhile, Murray makes an album deal for the band.
